title: Fondant cake covering
subtitle: Rolling and applying sugarpaste to a celebration cake
excerpt: A technique for covering a layered cake with a smooth coat of rolled fondant (sugarpaste). The cake is first coated with apricot glaze or buttercream to give the fondant something to adhere to, then the fondant is rolled out, draped over the cake, and smoothed down.
sourceNotes: Covering celebration cakes with rolled sugarpaste became standard in British cake decorating in the 20th century. The technique is documented in British sugarcraft guides from the 1950s onward and is the foundation of tiered wedding cake decoration. The apricot glaze undercoat, which provides a sticky surface for the fondant to adhere to, is a British cake decorating standard documented in period confectionery guides.

recipe.yieldDescription: covering for a 20 cm round cake
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fondant-covered cakes can be made 3 days ahead. Keep at room temperature; refrigerating causes condensation on the fondant surface.

glossaryTerms.definition: Once the fondant is draped over a cake, it must be smoothed from the top centre outward and down the sides, working quickly before the fondant begins to set. Use the palms of the hands or a cake smoother tool, applying even pressure and stretching the fondant gently around the curves. Work from the top, then the upper sides, then press the fondant against the lower sides and trim the excess at the base. Air bubbles trapped under the fondant are released by piercing with a clean pin and smoothing over.

content.text: Covering a cake with fondant produces the clean, smooth finish associated with celebration and wedding cakes. The process has two stages: preparing the cake surface so the fondant adheres, and rolling and applying the fondant without tearing it.
is easier if the fondant is worked at room temperature and the room is not too warm.

attrs.prepNote: for a 20 cm round cake, 8 to 10 cm tall

content.text: The cake should be cold, flat-topped, and crumb-coated with a thin layer of buttercream or apricot glaze to give the fondant a surface to adhere to. Brush the entire outside of the cake with warm, sieved apricot jam using a pastry brush, working over the top and all the way down the sides. The jam acts as a glue.

Knead the fondant until smooth and pliable. Dust the work surface lightly with icing sugar. Roll the fondant into a circle large enough to cover the top and sides of the cake with 5 cm extra on all sides — for a 20 cm cake that is 8 cm tall, roll to about 40 cm in diameter and 4 mm thick. Roll the fondant loosely around the rolling pin to lift it, then unroll it centrally over the cake. Smooth the top gently outward from the centre, then ease the fondant down the sides without pleating, lifting and smoothing as you work around the cake.

Once the fondant is draped smoothly over the sides, use the palms of your hands or a cake smoother to press and smooth the surface, working in circular motions on the top and in downward strokes on the sides. Trim the excess fondant at the base with a sharp knife or palette knife held flat against the board. If air bubbles appear, pierce them with a clean pin and smooth over.
